food_subgroup: Indo-Chinese vegetable dish; deep-fried vegetable balls in spiced soy-based sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Veg Manchurian. FOODON_03302051 (vegetable dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Indo-Chinese Veg Manchurian. A0CKP (cooked mixed vegetable preparations) is the closest defensible parent. The dish involves deep-fried vegetable balls in a soy-ginger-garlic sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Veg Manchurian or a sufficiently close Indo-Chinese vegetable dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Veg Manchurian is approximately 250 g, comprising 6–8 deep-fried vegetable balls coated in a spiced soy-based sauce, typically served as a starter or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Manchurian — nutrient composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central ingredient components and ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for comparable Indo-Chinese vegetable preparations
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Veg Manchurian exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cabbage (FDC ID 169975), carrot (FDC ID 170393), capsicum (FDC ID 170108), all-purpose flour (FDC ID 169761), cornstarch (FDC ID 169715), soy sauce (FDC ID 175139), and vegetable oil (FDC ID 172336), combined with ICMR-NIN 2017 reference values for comparable deep-fried Indo-Chinese vegetable ball preparations. Estimated recipe composition: ~40% vegetable mix, ~20% batter (flour + cornflour), ~15% sauce, ~25% absorbed frying oil contribution. Values represent a mid-range estimate for the gravy variant.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable and batter-fried preparations — analogous estimate based on deep-fried vegetable fritters and wheat-flour-based coated vegetable dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Veg Manchurian in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous deep-fried wheat-flour-battered vegetable preparations. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values of 50–60 for mixed vegetable fritters and battered vegetable dishes with comparable carbohydrate composition. The sauce component (soy sauce, ketchup, cornflour thickening) contributes additional rapidly digestible carbohydrates. GL per 250 g serving = (55 × 46.25 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 25.4375, rounded to 25.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1200 mg per serving) from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Veg Manchurian to occasional consumption and request reduced-soy-sauce preparation, as a single serving can contribute approximately 50–60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) but moderate glycemic load (~25.4 per serving) from wheat-flour batter and cornflour-thickened sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Veg Manchurian in small portions, as the wheat-flour batter and cornflour sauce contribute a low-to-moderate glycemic load; pairing with a high-fibre side and monitoring post-meal blood glucose is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat from deep-frying (~9.5 g per 100 g) and high sodium from soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Veg Manchurian due to its elevated sodium content and fat from deep-frying, both of which can adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles; an air-fried or baked variant with low-sodium soy sauce is preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg Manchurian is a popular Indo-Chinese fusion dish originating in India, widely consumed across South Asia and in South Asian diaspora communities including Singapore. It consists of small deep-fried balls made from finely chopped mixed vegetables — typically cabbage, carrot, and capsicum — bound with all-purpose flour and cornflour, then coated in a spiced sauce made from soy sauce, ginger, garlic, green chilli, tomato ketchup, and vinegar. The dish is served either dry (tossed in sauce) or as a gravy (with a thicker sauce base). The texture is crispy on the outside and soft inside, with a bold umami-sweet-spicy flavour profile. Nutritionally, Veg Manchurian is moderate in calories (~165 kcal per 100 g) with significant fat from deep-frying (~9.5 g) and notably high sodium (~480 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce. It provides modest dietary fibre (~2.1 g) and vitamins A and C from the vegetable content. The dish is fully vegetarian and vegan, and is halal-compliant. Clinically, the high sodium content is a concern for hypertension management, and the low glycemic index (estimated ~55) with moderate glycemic load requires portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish appears on menus at Turkish and Lebanese restaurants in Singapore that also serve Indo-Chinese items, reflecting the multicultural food landscape of the city-state.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Veg Manchurian?
Is Veg Manchurian high in sodium?
What is the difference between dry and gravy Veg Manchurian?
What vegetables are in Veg Manchurian?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Veg Manchurian exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition using USDA SR Legacy and ICMR-NIN 2017 reference data for comparable preparations. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar deep-fried battered vegetable dishes. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and ingredient-level sources are approved, but direct measurement data is absent.
